questions.question:
Relative mode of addressing is most relevant to writing:
A. Co-routines
B. Position-independent code
C. Shareable code
D. Interrupt Handlers
questions.to_find: Which programming construct benefits most from relative (PC-relative) addressing?

understand.plain: Relative (PC-relative) addressing computes the effective address by adding an offset to the current Program Counter (PC). Because all addresses are expressed as displacements from the PC rather than as fixed absolute addresses, the code works correctly no matter where in memory it is loaded — this is called position-independent code.

keywords.explain: The effective address is computed as PC + offset; no absolute address is embedded in the instruction.
keywords.example: JUMP +4 means jump to the instruction 4 bytes ahead of the current PC, regardless of where the program is loaded.

keywords.explain: Code that executes correctly regardless of its absolute load address, because it uses only relative addresses.
keywords.example: Shared libraries (.so / .dll) are compiled as PIC so they can be mapped anywhere in any process's address space.

understand.visual_alt: PC-relative addressing: effective address = PC + signed offset, so the code is relocatable.

given.aim: Identify which programming task requires relative addressing mode.

terms.meaning: Register holding the address of the next instruction to be executed.
terms.example: If PC = 0x1000, a +4 offset resolves to 0x1004.
terms.connects: Relative addresses are offsets added to PC, making code independent of its load address.

terms.meaning: The instruction encodes the exact memory address of the operand or jump target.
terms.connects: Code using absolute addresses must be relocated (patched) if loaded at a different address.

terms.meaning: Code that uses only relative or indirect addressing so it works at any load address.
terms.example: Shared libraries loaded by the OS at runtime must be PIC.
terms.connects: PC-relative addressing is the primary mechanism enabling PIC.

given.plan: Recall that PC-relative addressing encodes targets as offsets from the PC. This means the code does not contain absolute addresses and can run at any location in memory — the defining property of position-independent code.

steps.title: Understand relative addressing
steps.apply: In relative mode, the operand/branch target is specified as a signed offset from the current PC. The absolute address is never stored in the instruction.
steps.note: This is the mode used for branches in RISC architectures like ARM and RISC-V.

steps.title: Match to the correct option
steps.apply: Co-routines require indirect or register addressing for context switching. Shareable code needs read-only data, not necessarily relative addressing. Interrupt handlers use fixed vectors (absolute). Only position-independent code fundamentally relies on relative addressing to avoid hardcoded absolute addresses.
steps.note: Shared libraries are a classic real-world example of position-independent code compiled with PC-relative branches and data references.

questions.formula_note: PC-relative addressing: Effective Address = PC + offset; key enabler of position-independent code.

questions.question:
Which of the following addressing modes permits relocation without any change whatsoever in the code?
A. Indirect addressing
B. Indexed addressing
C. Base register addressing
D. PC relative addressing
questions.to_find: Which addressing mode allows code to be relocated to a different memory region without modifying any instruction?

understand.plain: Relocation means loading a program at a different base address than it was compiled for. If instructions reference memory using absolute addresses, those addresses must be patched at load time. PC-relative addressing avoids this because all addresses are encoded as offsets from the current PC; no instruction contains an absolute address, so nothing needs to change when the program is moved.

keywords.explain: Loading a program at a memory address different from the one assumed during compilation.
keywords.example: A program compiled to run at 0x0000 is loaded at 0x4000 instead.

keywords.explain: Effective address = PC + offset; the offset is fixed at compile time and remains valid regardless of the absolute load address.
keywords.example: A branch instruction JUMP -8 always jumps 8 bytes backward from wherever it currently executes.

understand.visual_alt: When a program is relocated, PC-relative offsets between instructions stay constant, so no code patching is needed.

given.aim: Identify the addressing mode that is inherently relocatable without any code modification.

terms.meaning: The instruction gives an address that holds the actual address of the operand (double indirection).
terms.example: LOAD [1000] fetches the value at the address stored in location 1000.
terms.connects: The pointer stored in memory may need updating on relocation.

terms.meaning: Effective address = base address in instruction + value in index register.
terms.connects: The base address in the instruction is absolute and must be patched on relocation.

terms.meaning: Effective address = base register + offset; the OS loads the base register with the actual load address.
terms.connects: Relocation only requires updating the base register, not the code — but requires hardware/OS support to set the register.

terms.meaning: Effective address = PC + signed offset; all references are relative to the current instruction.
terms.example: BRANCH +4 always moves 4 bytes forward regardless of load address.
terms.connects: No absolute address appears anywhere in the code, so zero modification is needed on relocation.

given.plan: Compare each mode's use of absolute versus relative addresses. Only PC-relative addressing avoids absolute addresses entirely, making relocation transparent with zero code changes.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: Indirect addressing: pointer values stored in memory contain absolute addresses — must be patched. Indexed addressing: base address embedded in instruction is absolute — must be patched. Base register addressing: base register must be updated by the loader/OS (hardware change, not code change, but it is still a 'change' in setup). PC-relative addressing: every address is PC + offset; moving the entire program preserves all relative distances.
steps.note: The question asks for 'no change whatsoever in the code' — only PC-relative satisfies this completely.

steps.title: Select the correct answer
steps.apply: PC-relative addressing encodes all jumps and data references as displacements. When the program is relocated, every instruction and its target move by the same amount, so the displacement (offset) remains unchanged.
steps.note: This is why modern shared libraries and PIC executables use PC-relative addressing by default.

questions.formula_note: PC-relative addressing: EA = PC + offset; displacements are invariant under relocation, requiring zero code modification.

questions.question:
A certain processor supports only the immediate and the direct addressing modes. Which of the following programming language features cannot be implemented on this processor? (Choose all that apply)
A. Pointers
B. Arrays
C. Records
D. Recursive procedures with local variables
questions.to_find: Which high-level language features require addressing modes beyond immediate and direct?

understand.plain: Immediate mode provides a constant value directly in the instruction. Direct mode accesses a fixed, known memory address. Both are resolved at compile time. Features like pointers and recursive procedures require computing addresses at run time — pointer dereferencing needs indirect addressing, and recursion's stack-allocated locals need base+offset or indirect addressing. Arrays and records can be handled with compile-time-fixed addresses (static arrays/records), but pointers and recursion fundamentally need dynamic address computation.

keywords.meaning: The operand value is embedded directly in the instruction.
keywords.example: ADD R1, #5 adds the constant 5 to R1; no memory access needed.

keywords.meaning: The instruction specifies the exact memory address of the operand.
keywords.example: LOAD R1, 0x2000 loads the value at address 0x2000 into R1.

keywords.meaning: The instruction provides an address whose content is the effective address (pointer dereference).
keywords.example: LOAD R1, (R2) loads the value at the address stored in R2 — essential for pointers.

understand.visual_alt: Pointers need indirect (register-indirect) addressing; recursive locals need stack/base-offset addressing. Both are absent on a processor with only immediate and direct modes.

given.aim: Determine which features require addressing modes not available (indirect, register-indirect, base+offset).

terms.meaning: Variables that store memory addresses; dereferencing a pointer requires reading the address from a register or memory location at run time.
terms.example: int *p = &x; *p = 5; — the address of x is only known at run time.
terms.connects: Pointer dereference requires indirect/register-indirect addressing, which this processor lacks.

terms.meaning: Each recursive call creates a new stack frame; local variables live at runtime-determined stack offsets.
terms.example: int fact(int n){ int result = n * fact(n-1); } — 'result' is at SP+offset, which changes each call.
terms.connects: Accessing stack-frame locals requires base-register or stack-relative addressing, not just direct addressing.

terms.meaning: If arrays and records are allocated statically (global/fixed addresses), each element has a known compile-time address.
terms.example: int a[5]; — elements at fixed addresses a, a+1, a+2, ... can all be accessed via direct mode.
terms.connects: Static arrays/records can be implemented with direct addressing alone.

given.plan: Check whether each feature requires run-time address computation. Pointers need indirect addressing (run-time address). Recursion needs dynamic stack-frame access (base+offset). Static arrays and records use fixed addresses reachable via direct mode.

steps.title: Analyze Pointers
steps.apply: A pointer holds an address computed at run time. Dereferencing it means: go to the address stored in this variable and read/write there. This requires indirect (register-indirect) addressing. The processor only has immediate and direct modes — neither can dereference a run-time computed address. Therefore, pointers CANNOT be implemented.
steps.note: Even if you store the pointer value at a direct address, you still need indirect mode to use it.

steps.title: Analyze Arrays
steps.apply: A statically allocated array has elements at fixed, compile-time-known addresses. Each element can be accessed via direct addressing (LOAD arr[0], LOAD arr[1], etc.). Dynamic/heap arrays would need indirect mode, but statically declared arrays CAN be implemented with direct addressing.
steps.note: GATE 1999 answer treats arrays as implementable with direct mode for static allocation.

steps.title: Analyze Records (Structs)
steps.apply: A statically allocated record (struct) has fields at fixed, compile-time-known offsets from a known base address. All field accesses reduce to direct addressing. Records CAN be implemented with direct addressing.
steps.note: Dynamic records (heap-allocated) would require indirect mode, but statically allocated records do not.

steps.title: Analyze Recursive Procedures with Local Variables
steps.apply: Recursion creates multiple active instances of a procedure simultaneously. Each instance's local variables live in a new stack frame at run-time-determined addresses (SP + offset). Accessing them requires base-register or stack-relative addressing. With only immediate and direct modes (fixed addresses), you cannot access locals whose addresses are only known at run time. Therefore, recursive procedures with local variables CANNOT be implemented.
steps.note: Non-recursive procedures with only static locals could use direct addressing, but recursion with local variables cannot.

solution.result: A (Pointers) and D (Recursive procedures with local variables) cannot be implemented.

questions.formula_note: Pointer dereference requires indirect addressing; recursive stack-frame local access requires base-register or stack-relative addressing — neither available when only immediate and direct modes exist.

questions.question:
The most appropriate matching for the following pairs is:

X: Indirect addressing    1: Loops
Y: Immediate addressing   2: Pointers
Z: Auto decrement addressing  3: Constants

A. X - 1, Y - 3, Z - 2
B. X - 3, Y - 2, Z - 1
C. X - 2, Y - 3, Z - 1
D. X - 3, Y - 1, Z - 2
questions.to_find: Match each addressing mode to the programming construct it is most naturally used for.

understand.plain: This question tests whether you can associate each addressing mode with its primary use case. Indirect addressing uses an address stored in a register or memory location to reach the actual operand — exactly what pointers do. Immediate addressing embeds a literal value in the instruction — perfect for constants. Auto-decrement addressing automatically decrements a register before using it as an address — the classic mechanism for stack-based loops and iteration.

keywords.explain: The instruction holds an address (or register) whose content gives the effective address; supports pointer semantics.
keywords.example: LOAD R1, (R2) — R2 holds a pointer; this reads the value R2 points to.

keywords.explain: The operand is a literal constant embedded directly in the instruction word.
keywords.example: ADD R1, #10 — adds the constant 10; no memory access needed.

keywords.explain: A register is automatically decremented (pre- or post-) each time it is used as an address, naturally stepping through a sequence of memory locations.
keywords.example: Used in stack push/pop and loop counters: MOVE -(R1), R2 decrements R1 then stores R2.

understand.visual_alt: X (Indirect) -> 2 (Pointers): pointer dereference is indirect addressing. Y (Immediate) -> 3 (Constants): literal values are immediate operands. Z (Auto-decrement) -> 1 (Loops): auto-decrement steps through memory/counts iterations.

given.aim: Correctly match three addressing modes to the high-level programming constructs they implement.

terms.meaning: Accesses memory via a pointer — the instruction specifies an address that contains the real address.
terms.example: *ptr = 5 in C compiles to an indirect store through the pointer register.

terms.meaning: Provides a fixed numeric value encoded within the instruction itself.
terms.example: int x = 42; — the literal 42 is an immediate operand in the MOV instruction.

terms.meaning: Decrements a register before/after using it as an address, enabling automatic traversal of arrays or iteration counts.
terms.example: A for-loop counting down uses DJNZ (decrement and jump if not zero) — an auto-decrement pattern.
terms.connects: Directly implements loops (option 1) via automatic counter manipulation.

given.plan: Map each mode to its primary use: Indirect -> Pointers (both involve following an address), Immediate -> Constants (literal value in instruction), Auto-decrement -> Loops (automatic counter step). The only option matching X-2, Y-3, Z-1 is C.

steps.title: Match Indirect addressing
steps.apply: Indirect addressing fetches the effective address from a register or memory location — exactly how pointers work in high-level languages. X matches 2 (Pointers).
steps.note: In C, *p = 5 performs an indirect store through pointer p.

steps.title: Match Immediate addressing
steps.apply: Immediate addressing provides a literal numeric value directly in the instruction. This is how the compiler handles constants. Y matches 3 (Constants).
steps.note: Example: ADDI R1, R1, 5 adds the constant 5; no memory access needed.

steps.title: Match Auto-decrement addressing
steps.apply: Auto-decrement (and auto-increment) addressing automatically adjusts a register each time it is used as a pointer, which is the natural mechanism for loop counters and iterating through arrays. Z matches 1 (Loops).
steps.note: Stacks also use auto-decrement for push. The DEC-and-branch pattern is fundamental to loops.

steps.title: Select answer
steps.apply: X-2, Y-3, Z-1 corresponds to option C.
steps.note: Check options: A gives X-1,Y-3,Z-2 (wrong — indirect is not loops); B gives X-3,Y-2,Z-1 (wrong); D gives X-3,Y-1,Z-2 (wrong). Only C is correct.

questions.formula_note: Addressing mode-to-construct mapping: Indirect -> Pointers (EA via stored address), Immediate -> Constants (literal in instruction), Auto-decrement -> Loops (automatic register step).
